title: The Real Difference Between a B.A. and a B.S. Degree Explained
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You're filling out your college application. You select your major: Psychology. Then the dropdown asks: B.A. or B.S.? You freeze. You thought you had this figured out. Now you're wondering if you pick wrong, will it ruin your career? Probably not. But the choice does matter. Here is what actually changes between a Bachelor of Arts and a Bachelor of Science.

The Core Difference

A B.A. requires more liberal arts courses. A B.S. requires more technical and math courses. That's it. Same major. Same department. Different path through it.

A B.A. in Biology might require one semester of calculus. A B.S. in Biology might require two semesters of calculus plus statistics. The biology content is similar. The quantitative wrapper around it is not.

What a B.A. Typically Includes

More humanities electives. More flexibility. Often a foreign language requirement. Designed to give you a broader education. The idea is that you're studying a discipline within a wider context of human knowledge.

A B.A. in Computer Science might let you take philosophy of technology or ethics courses instead of differential equations. You still learn to code. You just spend less time on theory and more time on the implications of what you're building.

What a B.S. Typically Includes

More lab work. More quantitative analysis. Fewer electives outside the major. The curriculum is tighter. The sequence of courses is more rigid. You have less room to explore.

A B.S. in Psychology will push you toward statistics and research methodology. You might take cognitive neuroscience instead of developmental psych electives. The path is built to prepare you for research or clinical work.

Does It Affect Your Career?

Sometimes yes. Sometimes no. It depends on the field and the employer.

If you want to be a research scientist, a B.S. signals you have the quantitative training. If you want to go to medical school, either degree works as long as you complete the pre-med prerequisites. Medical schools care about your GPA and MCAT scores, not whether your diploma says Arts or Sciences.

In business, most employers won't ask. They'll see your major and your experience. The B.A. versus B.S. distinction rarely comes up in an interview. If you're applying to graduate school in a technical field, a B.S. might give you an edge. If you're applying to law school, a B.A. with strong writing and critical thinking skills might serve you better.

How to Choose

Look at the actual course requirements. Don't decide based on the label. Open the academic catalog. Compare the two tracks side by side. Which courses excite you more? Which ones do you dread? That's your answer.

If you want flexibility and a broader education, lean B.A. If you want depth and technical rigor, lean B.S. If you plan to double major, a B.A. often leaves more room in your schedule.

Common Misconceptions

A B.S. is not automatically more employable. A B.A. is not a softer version of the major. Some of the most prestigious programs in the country only offer B.A. degrees in fields like Physics and Computer Science. The distinction tells you about the curriculum structure, not the quality.

Don't let anyone tell you a B.A. is a consolation prize. And don't assume a B.S. will get you a higher salary. Your internships, your projects, and your network matter more than the two letters after your major.

The Bottom Line

Pick the path that matches how you want to learn. If you thrive on interdisciplinary connections, choose the B.A. If you want to drill deep into methodology and analysis, choose the B.S. Neither door closes off your future. Both can take you where you want to go. The difference is what you'll think about along the way.
